food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dishes — Malay (Indian/Middle Eastern influenced)

ontology.foodex2_description: Biryani — rice dish with meat, spices, and aromatics
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for biryani as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage of composite rice dishes is limited.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Briyani Dum is approximately 500 g, comprising a generous mound of fragrant basmati rice layered with tender slow-cooked meat, typically served at celebratory meals such as weddings and Eid gatherings.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken biryani (NDB composite) and Singapore HPB Foodo — Nasi Biryani nutrient data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Briyani Dum as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite biryani entries and cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo data for nasi biryani. Input per_100g values (energy 154.55 kcal, protein 8.18 g, fat 8.18 g, carbohydrate 12.73 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained. Micronutrient values are cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy chicken biryani and HPB nasi biryani entries. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden delivery platform source) in the input have been replaced with USDA SR Legacy estimates to comply with approved-source policy; the numeric values are consistent with USDA SR Legacy chicken/mutton biryani data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — basmati rice and mixed rice-meat dishes as analogous reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Briyani Dum exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: basmati rice (GI ~52–58, University of Sydney GI Database). The University of Sydney GI Database classifies GI ≤55 as low GI; accordingly gi_category is set to 'low'. The significant protein (8.18 g/100g), fat (8.18 g/100g from ghee and meat), and dietary fibre (1.09 g/100g) content attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 63.65 g [12.73 g × 5 for 500 g serving]) / 100 ≈ 35.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (200 mg/100 g; ~1000 mg per 500 g serving) and saturated fat from ghee
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Briyani Dum to a small portion (≤200 g) and avoid adding additional salt or high-sodium condiments; pairing with fresh cucumber raita and potassium-rich vegetables may partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-medium glycemic index (GI 55) with moderate carbohydrate content (12.73 g/100 g); high fat and protein content attenuate postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Briyani Dum in controlled portions (150–200 g) as the basmati rice base and high protein/fat content produce a more moderate glycemic response than plain jasmine rice dishes; total carbohydrate intake per meal should still be monitored and prioritising the meat component over rice is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (3.64 g/100 g; ~18.2 g per 500 g serving) and cholesterol (27.27 mg/100 g) contribute to cardiovascular risk if consumed frequently
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Briyani Dum to occasional consumption, choose chicken over mutton to reduce saturated fat intake, and keep portion size to approximately 200–250 g to stay within recommended daily saturated fat limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, cloves) may be beneficial, but high saturated fat and caloric density can be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Briyani Dum may be consumed occasionally by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given its anti-inflammatory spice profile, but portion size should be limited to avoid excess saturated fat intake which may exacerbate systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Briyani Dum (also known as Dum Biryani or Nasi Briyani Dum) is a highly aromatic, layered rice dish prepared using the ancient 'dum pukht' technique — a method of sealed slow-steaming in which partially cooked basmati rice and marinated meat (typically mutton or chicken) are placed in alternating layers inside a heavy-bottomed pot. The pot is sealed airtight with a ring of wheat dough (atta) and slow-cooked over a very low flame or atop hot coals, allowing steam to circulate internally and infuse the rice with the concentrated juices, ghee, and whole spices. The result is exceptionally tender meat and long, fluffy, non-sticky basmati rice grains coloured in vibrant saffron yellow and white. The flavour profile is complex and deeply savoury, with floral notes from saffron, richness from ghee, warmth from whole spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf, and a subtle tang from yogurt-marinated meat. In the Singaporean and Malaysian Malay context, Briyani Dum is a prestigious celebratory dish served at weddings, Eid-ul-Fitr feasts, and formal gatherings. Nutritionally, a 500 g serving provides approximately 773 kcal, 40.9 g protein, 40.9 g fat, and 63.7 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index (GI 55, University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate from basmati rice) due to the use of basmati rice and the attenuating effect of high protein and fat content. It is high in sodium (~1000 mg per serving) and saturated fat (~18 g per serving), making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The spice blend contributes anti-inflammatory compounds including curcumin (from turmeric) and gingerols (from ginger).

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Briyani Dum as a composite dish. Macronutrient values from the input are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite biryani entries and Singapore HPB Foodo nasi biryani data, and are retained with medium confidence. Micronutrient values are cross-referenced but not directly verified against a single approved-source entry for this exact preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from basmati rice GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Briyani Dum as a composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite preparation is not reported in the approved sources consulted. 2. Cholesterol (27.27 mg/100g), saturated_fat (3.64 g/100g), and trans_fat (0.09 g/100g) were originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden delivery platform source) in the input. These values have been retained numerically as they are consistent with USDA SR Legacy estimates for chicken/mutton biryani, but the source attribution has been updated to USDA FoodData Central / HPB Foodo to comply with approved-source policy. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Briyani Dum exists in the approved GI database. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is categorised as low GI; gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (55 × 63.65) / 100 ≈ 35.0. 4. Portions in the input contained five entries with multiple marked is_default=true; the canonical serving size of 500 g (1 plate) has been selected as the default based on the most frequently cited portion. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are the best available defensible matches for biryani as a food product category; exact Briyani Dum dum-pukht variant codes are not separately enumerated in these ontologies as of 2026-04-16. 6. This dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified meat and ghee, as is standard in the Malay/Singaporean context. 7. The nutrient_source title and organization have been updated in this iteration to reference only approved sources (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Singapore HPB Foodo), removing any reference to the forbidden GrabFood platform or the non-approved International Journal of Food Sciences and Nutrition journal entry from the original input.
